title: AlloyDB Ships Proxy Models That Replace LLM Calls with Local Inference Inside the Database
gist: Google made AlloyDB AI functions GA, letting developers call LLMs directly in SQL, plus two acceleration layers: smart batching (2,400x throughput) and optimized proxy models (23,000x throughput, 6,000x cost reduction per Google's internal testing). Proxy models distill a frontier LLM's judgment into a lightweight local model trained on a data sample, inverting the database-to-LLM relationship.

insights.claim: AlloyDB AI functions let developers call LLMs directly within standard SQL queries, including ai.generate, ai.if, ai.rank, ai.forecast, plus new ai.summarize, ai.agg_summarize, and ai.analyze_sentiment operators.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Databases embedding LLM inference as SQL operators blurs the DB/ML boundary—a competitive move that pressures separate vector-DB and application-layer AI tooling.
insights.quote: a query can filter rows by meaning rather than rigid keyword matches

insights.claim: AlloyDB's proxy model uses a two-phase workflow: a PREPARE statement trains a lightweight local model from a data sample labeled by a frontier LLM, then queries execute against the local proxy, falling back to the frontier model when confidence is low.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Query-time distillation is the architecturally significant pattern—it turns the LLM from a per-row runtime dependency into a one-time teacher, slashing inference cost.
insights.quote: the database becomes a student that learns the model's judgment on a sample, then applies that judgment locally at database speed. The LLM becomes a teacher rather than a runtime dependency.

insights.claim: Per Google's internal testing, smart batching delivers 2,400x throughput (up to 10,000 rows/sec) over row-at-a-time, and optimized proxy models reach 23,000x throughput (100,000 rows/sec) with a 6,000x cost reduction.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Headline numbers quantify the cost/latency wall of per-row LLM calls and the potential savings—though the author flags they are vendor internal-testing figures.
insights.quote: smart batching delivers a 2,400x improvement in throughput over row-at-a-time processing, while optimized proxy models push that to 23,000x with a 6,000x reduction in cost

insights.claim: The per-row LLM cost/latency wall (e.g., 100,000 products = 100,000 Vertex AI round trips each carrying the same system prompt) is the universal problem any database calling external models faces.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Frames a structural bottleneck that will force every AI-enabled database toward batching or distillation—shaping the roadmap of the whole category.
insights.quote: a table with 100,000 products means 100,000 round trips to Vertex AI
insights.locator: The problem with calling an LLM per row

insights.claim: The proxy-model pattern raises the question of whether competitors like Aurora, Azure SQL, CockroachDB, and PlanetScale will adopt distillation-at-query-time or leave users to build the logic in application code.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Flags a looming competitive dimension across the managed-database market—watch which rivals ship in-DB distillation as an AI differentiator.
insights.quote: The question is whether competitors (Aurora, Azure SQL, CockroachDB, PlanetScale) will adopt similar distillation-at-query-time approaches
insights.locator: This pattern has implications beyond AlloyDB

insights.claim: AlloyDB is positioning as a PostgreSQL-compatible database where structured queries, semantic search (via ScaNN index, up to 10 billion vectors), and LLM-powered analysis coexist in one SQL layer, plus a managed MCP server letting AI agents query it without running their own MCP infra.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Google is bundling structured, vector, and LLM workloads plus agent access into one Postgres-compatible layer—a consolidation play against standalone vector DBs and MCP infra.
insights.quote: structured queries, semantic search, and LLM-powered analysis coexist in the same SQL layer

insights.claim: The 23,000x and 6,000x figures come from internal testing, apply specifically to ai.if in preview (not GA, not all AI functions), and require enabling a non-default database flag, so teams should benchmark against their own data before committing.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Tempers the marketing numbers—the standout gains apply to a preview feature only, a due-diligence caution for buyers evaluating AlloyDB for production AI.
insights.quote: the 23,000x and 6,000x numbers come from internal testing, apply specifically to ai.if in preview

insights.claim: A Starburst architect recommended treating AlloyDB AI functions as governed database extensions rather than magic WHERE clauses, starting with read-heavy review workflows before writing model-derived fields back, and tracking model cost separately from query cost.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Practical adoption guidance—separate model cost tracking and read-first rollout—that governance-minded data teams should heed.
insights.quote: Treat them as governed database extensions, not magic WHERE clauses.
